data.title: Carney says Canada has no plans to pursue China free trade deal after Trump tariff threat
data.description:
Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada has no plans to go ahead with a free trade deal with China in the wake of a tariff threat from President Trump.

“We have no intention of doing that with China or any other nonmarket economy,” Carney said Sunday, according to The Associated Press. “What we have done with China is to rectify some issues that developed in the last couple of years.”

On Saturday, Trump warned of 100 percent tariffs on the U.S.’s northern neighbor in the case of it making a trade deal with China.

“If Governor Carney thinks he is going to make Canada a ‘Drop Off Port’ for China to send goods and products into the United States, he is sorely mistaken,” Trump said on his Truth Social platform.

“China will eat Canada alive, completely devour it, including the destruction of their businesses, social fabric, and general way of life,” the president added.

Earlier this month, Canada and China came to an initial trade deal in which tariffs on electric vehicles and canola were cut. Canada was going to let a maximum of 49,000 Chinese electric vehicles at a 6.1 percent tariff into their country, according the AP, and Beijing was set to bring down its tariff on its canola seed in March.

Throughout his second term, Trump’s tariff threats have ruffled feathers with U.S. allies like Canada and the European Union. The Trump administration’s relationship with its northern neighbor has also taken a blow recently over the president’s threat to acquire Greenland, which is a Danish territory.

Last week, Trump told Carney that Canada “lives because of the United States” after the Canadian leader defended Greenland amidst the president’s rhetoric about acquiring the Arctic island.

data.title: Trump to send border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota
data.description:
President Trump announced he is sending White House border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota on Monday evening in the wake of the second fatal shooting of a protester in Minneapolis.

“I am sending Tom Homan to Minnesota tonight. He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there,” Trump wrote in a post on TruthSocial. 

“Tom is tough but fair and will report to me,” Trump wrote.

In a separate post, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt wrote that Homan would be managing ICE operations on the ground “to continue arresting the worst of the worst criminal illegal aliens.”

She also wrote that Homan would be coordinating with separate federal officials investigating fraud in Minnesota involving state social services.

The announcement comes as the White House faces a backlash over Immigrations Customs Envorcement’s aggressive actions in Minnesota, which have now led to the deaths of two protestors.

Video on Saturday showed an ICE officer shooting demonstrator Alex Pretti in the back after an altercation in which Pretti, filming the ICE officers, sought to help a woman being pepper sprayed before he was also pepper sprayed.

Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem said Pretti had brandished the gun at federal officers, even though video footage showed he was holding his smart phone while filming them. He did not appear to draw the 9-mm semiautomatic handgun that he had concealed at his beltline and for which he had a permit.

Democrats and local officials have offered blistering criticism of ICE’s handling of the incident and the administration’s narrative, while some Republicans have also offered criticism while calling for an investigation.

In an interview with The Wall Street Journal, the president did not answer directly when asked twice whether he thinks the agent acted appropriately, even as members of his administration have defended the agent’s actions as self-defense.  

Trump in his post also referenced the Minnesota fraud controversy, which led Gov. Tim Walz to decide to not seek a third term. The fraud involved some in Minnesota’s Somalian population, and Trump has tied the issue to another of his favorite targets, Rep. Ilham Omar (D-Minn.), who in his Monday post he said had grown wealthy over the last few years.

data.title: Why Trump says he won't be at the Super Bowl this year
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Super Bowl LX won’t be a repeat of last year for several reasons. The Kansas City Chiefs and the Philadelphia Eagles won’t be competing for the Lombardi Trophy, for example.

Another familiar face will also not be at the Super Bowl: President Donald Trump.

He’s been at several sporting events in recent years, including last year’s Daytona 500 and the New York Yankees game on Sept. 11. Trump attended Super Bowl LIX, as well, becoming the first sitting president to do so.

Trump had traveled to Palm Beach, Florida, before flying to New Orleans for the game at the Caesars Superdome. He waved to fans, shook hands with Chiefs defensive tackle Chris Jones, and participated in a meet-and-greet with this year’s honorary coin toss participants, including family members of victims in the New Year’s attack in New Orleans, city police officers and other emergency personnel before heading to his suite.

He isn’t expected to be spotted in a suite at Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara, California, for Super Bowl LX next month, however.

While speaking with the New York Post last week, Trump said he will not attend this year’s Big Game.

“It’s just too far away. I would. I’ve [gotten] great hands [at] the Super Bowl. They like me,” he said, according to the outlet. “I would go if, you know, it was a little bit shorter.”

A direct flight from the District of Columbia to San Jose is a little over six hours, plus some drive time (Air Force One flies out of Joint Base Andrews, about 15 miles from the White House, while Levi’s Stadium is in Santa Clara, about five miles from San Jose International Airport).

For comparison, the recent flight Trump made to Switzerland takes about eight hours, though his flight took longer after Air Force One was forced to turn around for a “minor electrical issue.”

Super Bowl LX will kick off at 6:30 p.m. EST on Sunday, Feb. 8.

data.title: The Minneapolis ICE shooting threatens police nationwide
data.description:
The fatal shooting of a Minneapolis woman by an ICE officer this month should alarm every law enforcement professional in this country. I say this not because of how it will be adjudicated in court, but because of what the incident — and the immediate official response to it — represents for the trust and credibility that police depend on for legitimacy in the eyes of the public.

As a retired police lieutenant with more than 20 years of experience, I find it hard to imagine a worse scenario for our profession at this moment.  

History and, hopefully, a transparent, independent investigation will ultimately adjudicate the shooting itself. But law enforcement cannot ignore the glaring gap between what authorities have told the public and how people are interpreting the incident — a gap made wider by a pattern of opaque federal enforcement practices. We are now seeing routine scenes of masked officers separating parents from children, chaotic street-level arrests, and cases in which U.S. citizens have been wrongly detained. 

Though police in Minneapolis bear no direct responsibility for this week’s shooting, their department will nonetheless be left to manage the fallout. The incident is a reminder that trust in law enforcement is not owned by any single agency or jurisdiction. It is collective, built gradually through consistent, credible engagement, and easily shattered by perceived dishonesty, lack of accountability or misconduct.

The coming challenge for local police will be further complicated by the broader history and context leading up to this moment.

The Department of Homeland Security immigration operation launched in Minneapolis, described as the federal agency’s largest ever, did not occur in a vacuum. Police-community relationships in the city have been strained for years, most notably in the aftermath of George Floyd’s murder. More recently, Minneapolis has been singled out by national political figures as a symbol of disorder and defiance, reinforcing fears that law enforcement is being used to advance a political agenda rather than promote public safety. 

Just hours after the deployment, Renee Good, a 37-year-old mother of three who had been serving as a legal observer at a nearby immigration enforcement operation, was shot and killed in her car by an ICE officer. In its initial response, the Department of Homeland Security described the shooting as a response to an “act of domestic terrorism,” claiming Good had “weaponized” her vehicle. The language conveyed imminent danger and implied extraordinary justification for lethal force. But bystander video quickly called that narrative into question. 

Some officials have responded by urging patience and asking the public to wait for the investigative process to unfold. It is an understandable and well-intentioned impulse, but we must also acknowledge how the rising politicization of law enforcement has made that request far harder for many communities to accept. 

Concerns about aggressive federal intervention in cities are not new, and federal authorities have only heightened those worries with questionable attempts to punish ICE critics. In cities around the country, dozens of criminal cases against protesters have collapsed due to unreliable or false claims by federal officers.

In Chicago, the Justice Department recently dropped felony charges against a woman who had been shot five times after allegedly ramming an officer’s vehicle. Video evidence in that case contradicted the government’s account, and texts from the officer revealed that he had boasted about the shooting in the days afterward.

All of that context now has very real, on-the-ground consequences for police in Minneapolis. Large-scale demonstrations have already touched off in the city. Early reports indicate that city officers are directly bearing the anger and resentment of understandably outraged community members. How local police respond in the coming days and weeks will shape their relationship with the community for years to come.

The department has an opportunity — perhaps a narrow one — to rebuild and preserve trust by countering the perception that policing is serving partisan politics rather than the public. That starts with an unequivocal commitment to protect the right to protest, prioritizing de-escalation and constitutional policing over heavy-handed tactics, and demonstrating independence through transparency, accountability and a clear demand that federal agencies fully cooperate with an independent investigation.

That opportunity is already under strain. Federal authorities have reportedly blocked state investigators from the probe into Good’s killing, a step likely to deepen public suspicion just when transparency is most needed. For state and local authorities, the question now is whether they will accept being sidelined or insist on an investigative process the public can reasonably view as independent and fair.

I fear the long-term damage this incident may cause. Although an official determination may take months or even years, millions of people in Minneapolis and across the country are deciding right now what this moment means for their trust in law enforcement — and those judgments may prove far more consequential than any legal ruling.

Lt. Diane Goldstein (ret.) is a 21-year police veteran and executive director of the Law Enforcement Action Partnership, a nonprofit group of police, judges and other law enforcement professionals who support policies that improve public safety and police-community relations.

data.title: Man arrested in Maxwell Frost assault at Sundance Film Festival
data.description:
A man was arrested for allegedly assaulting Rep. Maxwell Frost (D-Fla.) during a Friday night party in Park City, Utah. 

Christian Joel Young, 28, was charged with aggravated burglary and assaulting an elected official, according to the Associated Press (AP). The outlet, citing court records, noted that he was transported to Summit County Jail.

Frost wrote about the incident on Saturday, in response to a Variety article stating that a Caucasian man punched Frost after talking about how proud he was to be white.

“Last night, I was assaulted by a man at Sundance Festival who told me that Trump was going to deport me before he punched me in the face,” Frost wrote on the social platform X Saturday. “He was heard screaming racist remarks as he drunkenly ran off. The individual was arrested and I am okay.”

Frost, 29, was the first Generation Z member of Congress upon his election in 2022. He was born in the United States.

County Judge Richard Mrazik ordered that Young, who has a prior misdemeanor conviction, be held without bail on the grounds that he poses “a substantial danger” if released, according to the AP.

House Democratic Leader Hakeem Jeffries (N.Y.) wrote on X Saturday that he is “horrified” by the incident. 

“Grateful that [Frost] is okay, but appalled that this terrifying assault took place. The perpetrator must be aggressively prosecuted,” Jeffries added. “Hate and political violence has no place in our country, and the entire House Democratic Caucus family stands with Maxwell.”

data.title: What Trump's risking in the row over Greenland
data.description:
Even for a president who has blown past all norms during his time in office, President Trump’s fight with NATO and Europe over Greenland is orders of magnitude more significant.  

Positively, Trump shut down a U.S. military invasion during his speech at Davos, announcing a “framework for a future deal” with Denmark, and generally taking a conciliatory tone. Similarly, his “Board of Peace,” while heavily weighted towards autocrats, at least demonstrates a desire to avoid future wars in the Middle East.

However, Trump pulled so forcefully at the strings holding NATO together that removing the threat of invasion or trade wars may not be enough to heal the damage done to America’s geopolitical, economic and security interests.

Further, Trump’s negotiating style is so heavy handed — by design — that it risks damaging America’s global standing and our foreign relations for years, all while playing into the hands of Russia and China. 

Perhaps the most bizarre aspect of the row over Greenland is that Denmark had already given the U.S. everything Trump ostensibly needs, short of sovereignty. We could already build additional military bases, mine rare earths and station components of the golden dome missile defense project there, all without annexing Greenland. Yet Trump kept pushing, and now the effects are showing up.

Indeed, on Tuesday, after a long weekend of escalating threats, U.S. bond yields spiked to their highest levels in months, directly hurting the administration’s focus on affordability. To be fair, the markets did bounce back immediately after Trump took invasion and tariff threats off the table, but a long-term loss of confidence in U.S. policy would be a genuine problem. 

Moreover, instead of working to bolster Republican support ahead of midterms, Trump was pursuing a foreign policy goal that has considerable opposition. Eighty-six percent of Americans opposed using the military to take control of Greenland, according to Quinnipiac polling. Further, just 29 percent — including 24 percent of always-critical independents — supported buying Greenland, according to a separate survey from Economist-YouGov. 

Add these together, and what comes out is an interesting finding by the New York Times-Siena poll, suggesting that Trump’s self-induced chaos is backfiring. Asked how Trump is handling relations with other countries, just 39 percent approved — 58 percent disapproved — a significant 19-point drop since he was inaugurated a year ago.

That said, the domestic blowback facing Trump pales in comparison to the consequences of his confrontation with NATO and Europe. Short term, Trump is telling our friends that the U.S. cannot be trusted. Likewise, even with the tariff threat subsiding, when the American president threatens trade wars with allies because he isn’t getting his way, it undermines the concept that the U.S. is a stable partner.  

There are also unforeseen consequences. On Wednesday, the European Union froze the U.S.-EU trade deal, which Trump had heralded. If the trade deal remains frozen, Americans will pay more for medication, cars and more. That is a losing strategy for the administration as midterms approach.

In that same vein, Trump’s shock-and-awe methods risk pushing allies closer to China. Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney recently “bent the knee” to Xi Jinping as the two inked a trade deal, and United Kingdom Prime Minister Keir Starmer is traveling to Beijing shortly.

Trump may be convinced that these countries cannot survive without the American economy, but Europe is not going to take his pressure campaign lying down. In the long term, threatening to cannibalize NATO is not only shortsighted, but the risks certainly outweigh the rewards of any deal over Greenland.

Economically, Trump risks forcing Europe to divest from the American economy, as Denmark hinted at with the sale of $100 million of U.S. treasurys. America’s economic supremacy will not fade overnight, but if Europe rapidly divorces from the U.S., the long-term economic consequences will be immense. A significant drop in foreign investment and diminishing of the dollar’s status would keep interest rates elevated, making things like homes much less affordable for Americans. 

Worse, the degradation of NATO would be the ultimate gift to Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping. In a world where “might makes right” and the U.S. has no friends, China and Russia win. The former would rapidly seize Taiwan, the way paved by Trump’s demolition of international norms.

For their part, Russia would find that Eastern Europe lay wide open, sparking the very world war that NATO was designed to deter. The Kremlin is already egging Trump on, stroking his ego with statements proclaiming that he will “go down in history” should he annex Greenland. 

Ultimately, even if Trump is driven by his desire to be seen as Thomas Jefferson or William McKinley — presidents who oversaw massive U.S. territorial expansion — ego-driven bluster, or a genuine concern for national security, he is going about securing U.S. interests in a way that could undermine those interests for generations. 

Douglas E. Schoen is a political consultant who served as an adviser to President Clinton and to the 2020 presidential campaign of Michael Bloomberg. He is the author of “The End of Democracy? Russia and China on the Rise and America in Retreat.”

data.title: Live updates: Minnesota judge to hear arguments on legality of immigration actions
data.description:
Another deadly shooting by federal immigration enforcement officials in Minneapolis has shaken the country, and it may push Washington to shut down part of the government later this week.

The killing of 37-year-old Alex Pretti by a Customs and Border Patrol agent on Saturday has renewed uproar nationally, and Senate Democrats have said they will vote against the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding package as Jan. 30 shutdown deadline looms.

Some Republican lawmakers are joining Democrats in calling for an impartial investigation into Pretti’s shooting.

A federal judge in Minneapolis will hear arguments Monday morning as the state of Minnesota sues the Trump administration, saying its immigration raid tactics are unconstitutional.

On Capitol Hill this week, the House is out, but the Senate is in, and it must pass spending bills to avert a government shutdown.

President Trump on Monday will hold policy meetings and do a radio interview. Press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters at 1 p.m.

Follow live below.

data.title: Racism is at the heart of Trump's immigration agenda
data.description:
President Trump’s immigration policy raises a multitude of questions, about civil and constitutional rights, federal versus state authorities, the tactical use of force by law enforcement, the proper use of the military, and more.

However, the most important question is about Trump’s true motivation. He says his goal is to purge the country of the “worst of the worst” immigrants who entered America illegally. That is an objective with which a substantial majority of Americans agree. But now, most Americans oppose how he is doing it.

The administration’s actions are far from surgical. Its violent tactics violate the laws and constitutional rights of citizens and non-citizens alike. They are meant to incite fear, discourage new immigrants from coming to America and encourage those already here to leave. When local officials and citizens push back against these tactics, Trump threatens to declare an insurrection and escalate the use of force.

It is difficult, then, to avoid the conclusion that Trump’s real agenda serves the white supremacists, white nationalists, neo-Nazis, accelerationists, and other hate-filled groups he has welcomed into his base. His goal is not to purge the nation of violent aliens; rather, it appears to be ethnic and racial cleansing.  

If he were really concerned about fixing America’s broken immigration system, Trump could sign the bipartisan immigration reform bill that Congress produced, and he scuttled, during the 2024 campaign. He could push for more resources for a legal immigration system so inefficient that immigrants are tempted to avoid it. Instead of spending tens of billions of dollars to expand Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention centers and hire 12,000 more ICE agents, Trump could hire more immigration judges to clear the backlog of nearly 4 million pending cases.

The White House has told masked and heavily armed ICE officers they have absolute legal immunity for their actions, which is not true. Their shock-and-awe tactics have resulted in hundreds of lawsuits. Now, with the U.S. Supreme Court’s blessing, skin color, broken English, vocation and tattoos have become probable cause for harassing and detaining Americans. ICE and Border Patrol agents appear to be following Trump’s plan to use U.S. cities as military training grounds.

Thirty-two people died in ICE custody in 2025, making it the agency’s deadliest year in more than two decades. Members of Congress have been barred from visiting ICE’s detention camps to check on how detainees are treated.

And the administration protects ICE from consequences. Although officers can be sued if their “conduct violates clearly established statute or constitutional rights,” the FBI’s refusal to investigate the fatal shooting of Renee Good signals that it will not investigate officers, let alone sanction them, even in wrongful death cases.

Trump paints immigrants with a broad, defamatory brush, frequently repeating his claim that they are rapists, gang members, drug dealers, murderers and mental patients who are “poisoning the blood” of the nation. He dehumanizes foreign nationals to justify policies that border on ethnic and racial cleansing. This is aligned with the far right’s Great Replacement Theory, which posits a conspiracy to replace the white population with non-white immigrants and lower white birth rates. 

Stephen Miller is widely regarded as the architect of Trump’s war on diversity. In 2019, the Southern Poverty Law Center analyzed more than 900 emails Miller sent to editors at Breitbart News, a conservative website, during Trump’s first presidential campaign. More than 75 Democrats in the House of Representatives and several congressional caucuses called for Miller’s resignation, saying his emails revealed “a racist and xenophobic worldview.” Instead, Miller has become one of Trump’s closest advisers.

PBS reported earlier this month that the administration’s campaign to recruit more ICE agents “draws on images and language borrowed from right-wing and white nationalist circles.” A Department of Labor message includes “One homeland, one people, one heritage. Remember who you are, American.”  An ICE recruitment ad uses the phrase,  “We’ll have our home again,” echoing a song popular among extreme right-wing groups. 

It is time to confront some fundamental questions. What kind of country are we? What kind do we want to be? Do we really believe that diversity “poisons” our blood instead of giving us strength? Are some of us worried that immigrants will do to us what our forebears did when they claimed that “manifest destiny” entitled them to steal the lands and destroy the cultures of the 7 million original Americans?

I believe our manifest destiny today is to prove that humanity can be united not by race but by shared ideals like those in our founding documents. Call me “woke,” but I think anything less cheapens the sacrifice of the nearly 3 million patriots who have shed blood in defense of that America.    

William S. Becker is co-editor of and a contributor to “Democracy Unchained: How to Rebuild Government for the People,” and a contributor to Democracy in a Hotter Time, named by the journal Nature as one of 2023’s five best science books. He previously served as a senior official in the Wisconsin Department of Justice. He is currently executive director of the Presidential Climate Action Project.

data.title: Republican calls for deeper investigation into latest Minnesota shooting growing
data.description:
A growing number of Republican lawmakers are calling for an impartial investigation into the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a Customs and Border Patrol (CBP) agent in Minneapolis on Saturday.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) wrote Sunday on the social platform X that the probe into the incident must be “thorough and impartial,” which is the “basic standard that law enforcement and the American people expect following any officer-involved shooting.”

“For this specific incident, that requires cooperation and transparency between federal, state, and local law enforcement,” the North Carolina Republican, who is not running for reelection, added. “Any administration official who rushes to judgment and tries to shut down an investigation before it begins are doing an incredible disservice to the nation and to President Trump’s legacy.”

Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), meanwhile, wrote on X Sunday that the shooting “should raise serious questions within the administration about the adequacy of immigration-enforcement training and the instructions officers are given on carrying out their mission.”

She also called for a “comprehensive, independent investigation” into the incident and the convening of congressional committees.

Pretti, a 37-year-old American citizen, was shot and killed after CBP agents pushed him to the ground on a Minneapolis street. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem asserted Saturday that Pretti assaulted the CBP agents and brandished a gun at them. 

Video of the incident, however, shows the man filmed agents as they pushed a woman to the ground, then attempted to assist her before CBP personnel surrounded him. After an agent appeared to retrieve Pretti’s firearm, for which he had a permit to carry, another agent fired multiple rounds at him. 

Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) called the incident “incredibly disturbing” on Saturday.

“There must be a full joint federal and state investigation,” he wrote on X. “We can trust the American people with the truth.”

“Enforcing our immigration laws makes our streets safer. It also protects our national security,” Sen. Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.) said in a post on X. “But we must also maintain our core values as a nation, including the right to protest and assemble.”

“I expect a prioritized, transparent investigation into this incident,” Ricketts added.

Drew Evans, the superintendent of the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension (BCA) — which investigates use-of-force incidents in the state — said Saturday that the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) has taken over the case and that “others outside of the state” have blocked his bureau from taking part in the investigation.

Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Attorney General Keith Ellison (D) said at a Sunday press conference that BCA investigators, who obtained a judicial search warrant, were denied access to the scene by federal officers.

Late Saturday, a federal judge blocked DHS from “destroying or altering” evidence at the scene of the shooting.

Also Saturday, Rep. Andrew Garbarino (R-N.Y.) formally requested that CBP Commissioner Rodney Scott, Immigration and Customs Enforcement Acting Director Todd Lyons and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services Director Joseph Edlow testify in front of the House Homeland Security Committee, which he chairs. 

“I take my oversight duties for [DHS] seriously, and Congress has an important responsibility to ensure the safety of law enforcement and the people they serve and protect,” the New York Republican said in a release. Committee staffers previously invited the three officials on Jan. 15. 

Rep. Michael McCaul (R-Texas), a member of the Homeland Security Committee, wrote on X Sunday that he is “troubled by the events that have unfolded in Minneapolis” and that a “thorough investigation is necessary — both to get to the bottom of these incidents and to maintain Americans’ confidence in our justice system.”

data.title: When Tucker Carlson stops asking questions
data.description:
In a recent interview with Florida gubernatorial candidate James Fishback (R), Tucker Carlson allowed a plainly false claim about constitutional law to pass without challenge.

Fishback asserted that, under Florida law, public-university students could be disciplined or expelled for criticizing Israel. That claim is not merely controversial; it is factually and legally incorrect.

The lapse was striking, particularly when contrasted with Carlson’s recent interview with Ted Cruz, in which Carlson pressed Cruz repeatedly on factual precision — even drilling him on Iran’s exact population. In contrast, when it came to First Amendment law affecting millions of American students, that scrutiny vanished.

Carlson’s posture reflects a broader pattern on his post-Fox News platform, where claims about Israel are often presented without the level of factual interrogation he typically applies elsewhere. In this case, he simply allowed the claim to stand, without clarification, skepticism, or correction, letting a false account of constitutional law go unchallenged.

Specifically, Fishback told Carlson that Florida’s adoption of the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance working definition of antisemitism makes it “against the law to criticize Israel” — that a student who calls Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a war criminal, for example, could be punished or expelled from a public university. Carlson responded affirmatively and moved on.

But this claim is obviously false. The statute Fishback is discussing does not ban criticism of Israel, nor could it constitutionally do so. The law merely adopts the working definition as a guidance tool for identifying discriminatory conduct in educational settings. In fact, the statute explicitly states that “criticism of Israel that is similar to criticism of any other country cannot be regarded as antisemitic.” And it creates no speech restrictions, no disciplinary mandates, and no criminal penalties.

More fundamentally, no state statute can override the First Amendment. Public universities are state actors, bound by longstanding Supreme Court precedent protecting political expression, including speech that is offensive, controversial, or deeply unpopular. Additionally, in Healy v. James, the Supreme Court affirmed that “the college classroom and its surrounding environs are peculiarly the ‘marketplace of ideas.'” Furthermore, in Papish v. Board of Curators, the court held that a public university may not punish students simply for the content of their expression.

This does not mean universities are powerless to address antisemitism — however it may be defined — but it does require a distinction between conduct and speech. The practical reason institutions need a working definition of antisemitism is that when discriminatory conduct is motivated by antisemitism, it can trigger enhanced penalties under hate crime statutes.

Physically blocking Jewish students from campus spaces, vandalizing Jewish property, or targeting students for intimidation or exclusion may constitute discriminatory conduct subject to university discipline and, in some cases, criminal law. That is specifically where Florida’s statute is designed to help institutions identify such conduct — not to police political debate.

Misrepresenting these frameworks as speech bans undermines their actual purpose. When claims about antisemitism are incorrect, like the fairytale of the boy who cried wolf, enforcement is weakened precisely when it is needed most.

Carlson’s failure to challenge Fishback on this point matters, because it spreads misinformation about fundamental rights. When a nationally influential interviewer allows an incorrect legal claim to stand, especially from someone of the stature of a gubernatorial hopeful, viewers are left with the impression that criticism of Israel is legally forbidden on Florida’s public university campuses. That belief is false, corrosive to public understanding of free speech, and detrimental when it comes to protecting Jewish students from real harm.

The issue here is not Israel policy, nor is it partisan alignment. It is media responsibility. Carlson has demonstrated that he is capable of aggressive factual interrogation when he chooses to deploy it. The decision not to do so in this case reflects selective skepticism, a willingness to challenge facts only when they conflict with his preferred narrative.

Robust debate about Israel, antisemitism, and their boundaries is both legitimate and necessary. But that debate must begin with an accurate understanding of what the law actually does. When false claims about speech restrictions go unchallenged, constitutional literacy suffers, and trust in both journalism and civil-rights enforcement erodes.

Alexander Mermelstein is a Los Angeles–based policy researcher whose work focuses on media narratives, antisemitism and the Middle East.
